metadata.seed: I get panic attacks which affect me. I can't breathe well. I feel like I'm gonna pass out and I'm jittery. I'm just very, very anxious whenever I think about them, right before I have to do oral presentations. During them, I just feel like I'm gonna keel over. Okay.

patient.query: I get panic attacks which affect me. I can't breathe well. I feel like I'm gonna pass out and I'm jittery. I'm just very, very anxious whenever I think about them, right before I have to do oral presentations. During them, I just feel like I'm gonna keel over. Okay.

automatic_thoughts: I can't breathe well
I feel like I'm gonna pass out
I'm gonna keel over

neutral: Anxiety about having a heart attack (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the fear of not being able to breathe and feeling like you might pass out is really distressing. What evidence do you have that this will actually happen when you speak in front of others? How have you handled similar situations in the past?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: automatic thoughts about suffocation and passing out
retrieved_concepts_used.label: unknown

patient.query: Yeah, it really does feel overwhelming when I think about it. I mean, I can’t point to concrete evidence, it just… feels real in the moment, you know? In the past, I’ve definitely avoided situations where I had to speak up or present—I just couldn’t shake the feeling that I’d embarrass myself. It’s like I get this knot in my stomach that makes it hard to focus.

triggers: situations where I had to speak up
automatic_thoughts: I’ll embarrass myself
it feels real in the moment

entailment: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the fear of embarrassing yourself is really weighing on you. What evidence do you have that speaking up will actually lead to that outcome? Maybe we could explore a few times when you did speak up and see how it actually turned out.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Fear of saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: Yeah, I guess when I think about it, those times I did speak up weren’t as bad as I imagined. But still, the thought of it just fills me with dread, like my mind jumps straight to the worst-case scenario. It’s hard to shake off that feeling, even if logically I know it’s not always true. I guess I just can’t help but worry about what everyone thinks of me.

automatic_thoughts: the thought of it just fills me with dread
my mind jumps straight to the worst-case scenario
I just can’t help but worry about what everyone thinks of me

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of speaking to people in authority (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the dread you feel is tied to a pattern of expecting the worst, even when you’ve seen that it’s not always that bad. That pattern can keep the worry alive, even when you know it’s not entirely accurate. What evidence do you have that people actually judge you harshly? Exploring that might help you see the situation from a different angle.

patient.query: I mean, I can’t really prove it, right? It’s just this voice in my head that tells me I’m not good enough or that I’ll mess up. I remember a couple of times when I felt really embarrassed, and it just sticks with me. But maybe it’s more about how I see myself, rather than what others actually think? It’s confusing, though. I wish I could just silence that voice.

automatic_thoughts: I’m not good enough
I’ll mess up
how I see myself

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like that inner voice has been weighing on you for a while, especially after those moments of embarrassment. What evidence do you have that this voice reflects reality, and can you think of times when you felt confident or were praised instead? How might you view those moments differently if you were talking to a supportive friend?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: Yeah, that inner voice really has been rough. I suppose I do remember some times when things went well or when people complimented me, but those moments feel so… distant. It’s like I push them aside when I’m feeling anxious. If I were talking to a friend, I’d probably tell them to focus on the positives. But when I think about myself, those good moments just feel like flukes or something. Like it’s only a matter of time before I slip up again

automatic_thoughts: good moments feel like flukes
it's only a matter of time before I slip up again

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re treating those good moments as luck and fearing a repeat of mistakes. What evidence supports that they’re just flukes, and what evidence suggests they reflect real strengths? If you could view them as genuine achievements, how might that change how you feel in anxious moments?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: it's only a matter of time before I slip up again
retrieved_concepts_used.label: automatic_thoughts

patient.query: That inner voice really gets to me. I think about those times when things went well, but they feel so far away, like they don’t count. When I’m anxious, it’s like I forget all the good stuff and only remember the embarrassing moments. If I were telling a friend, I’d say to focus on the positives, but when it comes to me, those good moments feel like flukes—like I’m just waiting to mess up again.

automatic_thoughts: those good moments feel like flukes
I’m just waiting to mess up again
I forget all the good stuff
only remember the embarrassing moments

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel that the good moments are fleeting and that you’re always on the brink of messing up again. What evidence do you have that those positive moments are just flukes? How might you view them differently if you considered them part of a broader pattern rather than isolated incidents?

patient.query: I guess I don’t really have solid proof that they’re flukes, it just feels that way, especially when I’m in that anxious state. It’s hard to see those good moments as part of a pattern when I’m so caught up in the fear of failure. I mean, maybe I just focus too much on the bad stuff, you know? It’s like I’m waiting for the other shoe to drop, so I can’t really appreciate those better times.

automatic_thoughts: I don’t really have solid proof that they’re flukes
It just feels that way
I’m so caught up in the fear of failure
Maybe I just focus too much on the bad stuff
I’m waiting for the other shoe to drop

llm_response.response: It sounds like anxiety is making it hard to see the good moments. What evidence do you have that these moments are just flukes? How might you notice patterns when you feel less anxious?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess it does cloud my view a lot. I don’t really have strong evidence that those good moments are flukes; it’s just how my mind interprets things when I’m anxious. When I’m calmer, I can sometimes remember those moments more clearly, but it’s like the fear just overshadows everything else. I wish I could hold onto those good feelings longer, but they just slip away when the anxiety kicks back in.

automatic_thoughts: good moments are flukes
fear overshadows everything else
I wish I could hold onto good feelings

llm_response.response: What kinds of moments do you notice when you feel calmer, and how do those compare to the moments you think are flukes? Can you recall any times when a positive feeling lingered even as anxiety rose? What might it look like to gently hold onto a good moment for just a few minutes when the fear starts to surface?

patient.query:
I think when I feel calmer, I notice little things like a good conversation with a friend or even just enjoying a nice day outside. Those moments feel genuine and real, but then when the anxiety comes back, it's like they fade away so quickly. I can remember times when I’ve laughed and felt connected, but then moments later, I’d be worrying about saying something stupid or messing things up. 

Holding onto those good feelings feels tough, like my mind almost pushes them away because it’s br

I might say something stupid
I will mess things up

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing how quickly those calm moments slip away when anxiety returns. Exploring that might help you see if there’s a different way to view those moments.

patient.query: It’s true, sometimes those calm moments do seem to vanish way too fast. I guess I don’t really have solid evidence that they always disappear. I can think of a few times where I felt good for a bit longer, like after some fun with friends or a good day at work. But those moments get overshadowed by the anxiety, and I feel like I’m just waiting for it to come crashing back. It's frustrating! I want to hold onto those good feelings, but it’s like
